insights.title: Probabilistic Inference
insights.summary: When multiple hypotheses are logically valid, evidence doesn't just confirm or deny — it SHIFTS probability. Some evidence is diagnostic (strongly favors one hypothesis), some is neutral, and some is misleading. Base rates and convergent independent evidence lines determine the most probable conclusion.
insights.transfer_rule: When evidence supports multiple conclusions, weight each piece by how DIAGNOSTIC it is — how much more likely the evidence would appear under one hypothesis than another. Convergent evidence from independent sources dramatically increases posterior probability.

cases.title: The Poisoned Philanthropist
cases.summary: Victoria Chen, 52, a wealthy tech philanthropist, collapses at her own charity gala after drinking champagne. Toxicology confirms thallium sulfate — a slow-acting poison administered 2–4 hours before symptoms. Two people at the gala had means, motive, and opportunity. BOTH are logically valid suspects. Your job is not to pick one — it's to RANK them by probability and show your evidence weighting.
evidence: Victoria collapsed at 22:15 after drinking champagne throughout the evening gala.
Toxicology report: thallium sulfate, ingested 2–4 hours before symptom onset (between 18:15 and 20:15).
Marcus Reed, Victoria's business partner, holds a chemistry degree from Stanford.
Marcus was seen near the champagne service table at approximately 19:30, briefly.
Marcus stands to inherit controlling shares of the company, worth approximately $200M.
However, Marcus and Victoria were about to close a joint venture worth $50M to Marcus personally — a deal that dies with Victoria.
Elena Cruz, Victoria's personal assistant for 7 years, was demoted 6 weeks ago after a dispute over expenses.
Elena was removed from Victoria's will 4 weeks ago.
Elena had exclusive access to Victoria's personal champagne glass throughout the evening — she managed Victoria's food and drink preferences.
Elena ordered 50g of potassium thallium sulfate online 3 weeks ago. Purchase confirmed in her order history.
Elena told police the chemical was 'for the rat problem in my apartment building.'
Building management confirms: no rat complaints were filed by any tenant in the past 12 months.
A person was seen near the kitchen around 20:00. Later confirmed to be regular catering staff.
Victoria drank from a personal bottle of champagne — she was allergic to sulfite preservatives in standard champagne.
Forensic criminology reference: in 82% of thallium poisoning cases, the perpetrator had sustained, repeated access to the victim's food or drink.
detective_questions: Both Marcus and Elena had means, motive, and opportunity. What DIFFERENTIATES them in terms of probability?
Which piece of evidence is most DIAGNOSTIC — i.e., which shifts probability the most?
Does Marcus's chemistry degree actually matter for this specific poison?
What does the base rate (82% sustained access) tell you?
Elena claims the thallium was for rats. What does the building record say?
What would Marcus LOSE by poisoning Victoria?
analysis_protocol: List ALL logically valid hypotheses (not just the obvious suspect).
For each piece of evidence, decide: is it DIAGNOSTIC (strongly favors one suspect), NEUTRAL (fits both), or MISLEADING?
Apply base rates: which type of perpetrator does the forensic literature favor?
Rank your hypotheses from MOST to LEAST probable, citing specific evidence weights.
Check for convergence: do multiple INDEPENDENT lines of evidence point the same direction?
Beware of cognitive traps: flashy evidence (chemistry degree, large inheritance) isn't always diagnostic.
scenarios: Marcus Reed (business partner) poisoned Victoria for inheritance
Elena Cruz (personal assistant) poisoned Victoria out of grudge
An unknown outsider targeted Victoria at the gala
The poisoning was accidental or self-inflicted

evidence_meta.text: Victoria collapsed at 22:15 after drinking champagne throughout the evening gala.
Toxicology report: thallium sulfate, ingested 2–4 hours before symptom onset (between 18:15 and 20:15).
Marcus Reed, Victoria's business partner, holds a chemistry degree from Stanford.
Marcus was seen near the champagne service table at approximately 19:30, briefly.
Marcus stands to inherit controlling shares of the company, worth approximately $200M.
However, Marcus and Victoria were about to close a joint venture worth $50M to Marcus personally — a deal that dies with Victoria.
Elena Cruz, Victoria's personal assistant for 7 years, was demoted 6 weeks ago after a dispute over expenses.
Elena was removed from Victoria's will 4 weeks ago.
Elena had exclusive access to Victoria's personal champagne glass throughout the evening — she managed Victoria's food and drink preferences.
Elena ordered 50g of potassium thallium sulfate online 3 weeks ago. Purchase confirmed in her order history.
Elena told police the chemical was 'for the rat problem in my apartment building.'
Building management confirms: no rat complaints were filed by any tenant in the past 12 months.
A person was seen near the kitchen around 20:00. Later confirmed to be regular catering staff.
Victoria drank from a personal bottle of champagne — she was allergic to sulfite preservatives in standard champagne.
Forensic criminology reference: in 82% of thallium poisoning cases, the perpetrator had sustained, repeated access to the victim's food or drink.

cases.hidden_truth: Elena Cruz poisoned Victoria. She purchased thallium sulfate under the guise of rat poison (building records show no rats), had exclusive sustained access to Victoria's personal champagne glass throughout the gala, and the forensic base rate (82% sustained access) overwhelmingly favors her. Marcus's chemistry degree is a red herring — thallium sulfate is commercially available and requires no special knowledge. Marcus would also lose $50M by killing Victoria, making his motive contradictory.
cases.false_narrative: Marcus Reed must be the poisoner because he has a chemistry degree, was seen near the champagne, and stands to inherit $200M.

contradictions.description: Marcus's chemistry degree seems incriminating, but thallium sulfate is commercially available — Elena bought it online with no chemistry training.
Marcus gains $200M in shares but LOSES a $50M deal — his financial motive is contradictory.
Elena's claim that the thallium was for rats is contradicted by building management records.

inference_traps.description: Marcus's chemistry degree is flashy but not highly diagnostic because the poison was commercially available.
trap_keywords: marcus is guilty because of his chemistry degree
chemistry degree proves marcus poisoned her
stanford chemistry means marcus did it

inference_traps.description: Brief proximity to the champagne table is weaker than Elena's sustained exclusive access to Victoria's drink.
trap_keywords: briefly near the table proves marcus did it
marcus near champagne is enough proof

required_concept_rules.name: Sustained access reasoning
required_concept_rules.description: Recognizes that sustained/prolonged access to the victim's drink is the key differentiator

required_concept_rules.name: Evidence weighting awareness
required_concept_rules.description: Distinguishes diagnostic evidence from neutral or misleading evidence

forbidden_patterns.description: Concluding Marcus is guilty solely because of his chemistry degree

insight_usage_rules.description: Filters out the misleading evidence (catering staff sighting, chemistry degree red herring)
Detects Elena's deceptive claim about rat poison

hypotheses.label: Marcus Reed Business Partner
key_weakening: Joint venture worth $50M dies with Victoria — poisoning is financially irrational
Only brief proximity to champagne — not sustained access
Thallium sulfate is commercially available — chemistry degree is irrelevant

hypotheses.label: Elena Cruz Personal Assistant
key_supporting: Exclusive sustained access to Victoria's personal champagne glass all evening
Purchased thallium sulfate online 3 weeks before the event
Lied about the purchase (no rats in building)
Recently demoted and removed from will — strong emotional motive
82% forensic base rate favors sustained-access perpetrators
key_weakening: 7-year working relationship — colleagues say she cared about Victoria

hypotheses.label: Unknown Outsider
key_weakening: Victoria drank from a personal bottle — outsider would need access to it
Thallium requires sustained administration — one-time opportunity insufficient

hypotheses.label: Accidental Poisoning
key_weakening: Thallium sulfate does not occur naturally in champagne
Deliberate purchase of the compound was confirmed

evidence_weights.explanation: Slow-acting poison requiring 2-4 hours of sustained access strongly favors someone with continuous proximity
Chemistry degree seems incriminating but thallium sulfate is commercially available — no special knowledge required
Brief one-time proximity to the champagne table is weak evidence compared to sustained exclusive access
The $50M deal dying with Victoria makes Marcus's motive contradictory — evidence AGAINST H1
Exclusive sustained access to Victoria's personal champagne glass is the single most diagnostic piece of evidence
Pre-meditated purchase of the exact poison used — direct physical evidence of planning
Building management contradicts Elena's cover story — evidence of deception about the purchase
Person near kitchen was confirmed catering staff — a red herring designed to suggest an outsider
The 82% base rate for sustained-access perpetrators is a strong Bayesian prior favoring Elena

probability_channels.base_rates: 82% of thallium poisoning cases involve a perpetrator with sustained access to the victim's food or drink — this is the strongest prior.
probability_channels.behavioral: Elena lied about the thallium purchase (no rats in building), and the purchase was made 3 weeks before the event — premeditation signal.
probability_channels.physical_evidence: Elena had exclusive access to Victoria's personal champagne glass all evening. Marcus had only one brief visit to the service table.
probability_channels.temporal: The 2-4 hour administration window matches Elena's continuous access throughout the gala, not Marcus's single brief proximity at 19:30.
probability_channels.convergence: Five independent evidence lines converge on Elena: (1) sustained access, (2) chemical purchase, (3) deceptive cover story, (4) recent grudge/motive, (5) forensic base rate.

cognitive_traps.name: availability_heuristic
cognitive_traps.description: Marcus's Stanford chemistry degree FEELS damning but is irrelevant — thallium sulfate is commercially available online. Elena actually bought it.

cognitive_traps.name: anchoring_on_magnitude
cognitive_traps.description: The $200M inheritance is attention-grabbing, but Marcus would LOSE a $50M active deal. The net financial logic actually weakens his motive.
